 {"id":"frankenredis-be8t.5","title":"Evaluate jemalloc/mimalloc as global allocator for fr-server","description":"# Evaluate Alternative Global Allocators

## What

The April 9 flamegraph shows malloc (3.13%) + cfree (5.70%) + _int_malloc
(2.42%) = ~11% of CPU spent in the glibc allocator. Redis uses jemalloc by
default, which is optimized for the many-small-allocation pattern of
key-value stores.

## Why

glibc malloc is a general-purpose allocator. For workloads with many small,
short-lived allocations (RESP frame parsing, response serialization, key
copies), specialized allocators can provide:
- Lower fragmentation
- Better cache locality (thread-local arenas)
- Faster small-allocation paths (slab allocators)
- Reduced lock contention (though FrankenRedis is single-threaded, allocator
  internal locks still exist in glibc)

## Candidates

### jemalloc (via tikv-jemallocator crate)
- Redis's default allocator
- Excellent for mixed-size workloads
- Well-tested in production Redis deployments
- ~200KB binary size increase

### mimalloc (via mimalloc crate)
- Microsoft's allocator, optimized for modern workloads
- Often faster than jemalloc for small allocations
- Lower memory overhead
- ~100KB binary size increase

## Implementation

Add to crates/fr-server/Cargo.toml:
```toml
[dependencies]
tikv-jemallocator = { version = "0.6", optional = true }
```

In main.rs:
```rust
#[cfg(feature = "jemalloc")]
#[global_allocator]
static GLOBAL: tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc = tikv_jemallocator::Jemalloc;
```

## Verification

Benchmark with and without the feature flag:
1. fr-bench all workloads (set/get/incr/mixed, p1 and p16)
2. Flamegraph comparison — allocator functions should drop from ~11% to <5%
3. Memory usage comparison under sustained load (100K keys, 1M operations)
4. No unsafe code introduction — the allocator crate handles this

## Note on #![forbid(unsafe_code)]

The global allocator crates use unsafe internally but present a safe API.
The #[global_allocator] attribute is safe Rust. The fr-server binary can
use this without violating the unsafe_code forbid on the library crates.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Benchmark comparison (jemalloc vs glibc) for all workloads
2. If improvement >5%, add jemalloc as default feature for fr-server
3. If improvement <5%, close as "not worth the dependency"
4. Memory usage not significantly increased (< 10% overhead)
5. All tests pass with the new allocator","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:51.149254321Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-13T17:42:54.521734457Z","closed_at":"2026-04-13T17:42:54.521456861Z","close_reason":"Completed evaluation.  {"id":"frankenredis-be8t.6","title":"Investigate redundant drop_if_expired/exists calls per command path","description":"# Investigate Redundant Expiry Checks Per Command

## What

In the April 9 flamegraph, Store::drop_if_expired is 4.45% and Store::exists
is 4.36% of CPU. These functions perform key lookups with expiry checks. The
question is whether multiple expiry checks fire for the same key within a
single command path.

## Hypothesis

A typical SET command path may traverse:
1. execute_frame_internal → dispatch
2. dispatch_argv → set handler
3. set handler calls self.entries.get/get_mut (which might call drop_if_expired)
4. set handler calls exists/drop_if_expired explicitly before insert
5. If SETEX/PSETEX: additional expire path

If drop_if_expired is called 2-3 times for the same key in the same command,
the second and third calls are wasted — the key can't re-expire between calls
within a synchronous single-threaded execution.

## Investigation Steps

1. Trace all call sites of drop_if_expired in fr-store/src/lib.rs
2. For each Store method (set_string, get_string, exists, etc.), count how
   many times drop_if_expired is called per logical operation
3. Identify redundant calls where the same key is checked multiple times
4. Propose consolidation: either check once at entry point, or use a
   "just checked" sentinel

## Design for Fix (if redundancy confirmed)

### Option A: Check-once wrapper
Each Store method that touches a key calls drop_if_expired once at the top
and marks the entry as "checked this tick." Subsequent calls within the same
now_ms timestamp skip the check.

### Option B: Caller-side dedup
Move the drop_if_expired call to the dispatch layer (execute_db_scoped_command)
and remove it from individual Store methods. The dispatcher pre-expires all
keys the command will touch, then the Store methods can assume keys are live.

### Option C: Lazy-only
Don't proactively expire at all in the per-command path. Only expire when a
key is accessed and found to be expired. This is Redis's actual behavior
(lazy expiry + periodic background expiry). The per-access check is just
"if expired, delete and return nil."

Recommended: Option C if conformance allows — it matches Redis most closely.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Investigation report identifying all redundant expiry check paths
2. If redundancy confirmed: fix reduces drop_if_expired CPU share
3. All expiry conformance tests still pass
4. TTL/PTTL commands still return correct values","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:51.258704240Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T23:49:27.509468176Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T23:49:27.509202824Z","close_reason":"Reduced redundant drop_if_expired lookups in SET/EXPIRETIME/XADD/LPOP/RPOP paths","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["expire","perf","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-be8t.6","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-be8t","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-09T20:26:51.258704240Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"frankenredis-be8t.7","title":"Phase 2 comprehensive re-baseline: flamegraph + benchmark + delta artifact after all optimizations","description":"# Phase 2 Comprehensive Re-Baseline

## What

After all Phase 2 optimization subtasks are complete (or a meaningful subset),
run a comprehensive benchmark suite + flamegraph capture and produce a final
delta artifact comparing Phase 2 results to both:
- The original April 7 baseline (frankenredis-zjii era: ~1200 ops/sec)
- The April 9 Phase 1 recovery baseline (~75K-90K ops/sec per-command)
- The Redis 7.2.4 reference numbers

## Steps

1. Clean build: cargo build --release -p fr-server -p fr-bench
2. Start server: ./target/release/frankenredis --port 6399 --mode strict
3. Run fr-bench for ALL workloads at pipeline=1 and pipeline=16:
   set, get, incr, lpush, lpop, hset, hget, mixed
4. Capture perf flamegraph (-e cpu-clock -F 999) under pipeline=16 load
5. Capture strace -c under pipeline=16 load (verify syscall reduction)
6. Compare numbers to all previous baselines
7. Write artifacts/optimization/phase2-final/DELTA_REPORT.md with:
   - Before/after table for all workloads
   - Flamegraph comparison (what changed, what's left)
   - Syscall count comparison
   - Per-optimization-lever attribution (which commit contributed what)
8. Update README.md benchmark section
9. Update baselines/ with new checked-in JSON artifacts

## Target Numbers

Based on the optimization opportunities identified:
- SET p1: >85K ops/sec (from 75K) — HashMap alone should get this
- GET p1: >95K ops/sec (from 90K) — should match or exceed Redis
- SET p16: >400K ops/sec (from 268K) — write coalescing is the lever
- GET p16: >600K ops/sec (from 434K) — same lever

## This Is the Final Gate

This bead should only be claimed after the high-impact subtasks are done.
It produces the evidence that justifies closing the epic.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Complete benchmark table for all workloads at p1 and p16
2. Flamegraph showing no single function >10% (healthy distribution)
3. Delta report artifact checked in
4. README updated with final numbers

 {"id":"frankenredis-bfp","title":"Wire CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples to eviction subsystem","description":"## Problem

fr-runtime/src/lib.rs lines 961-962:
- maxmemory_eviction_sample_limit: 16 (Redis default is 5, called maxmemory-samples)
- maxmemory_eviction_max_cycles: 4

These are initialized in ServerState::default but neither is wired to CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples. Our default (16) is also higher than Redis's default (5), which affects eviction behavior fidelity.

## What Redis Does

CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples <count> changes the number of keys sampled during each eviction cycle. Higher values give better eviction accuracy at the cost of more CPU.

## Implementation Plan

1. Change default from 16 to 5 (match Redis)
2. Handle CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples → update maxmemory_eviction_sample_limit
3. CONFIG GET maxmemory-samples → return current value

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: Fix default, add CONFIG SET handler","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:16.369290124Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:45:05.445385859Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:45:05.444890462Z","close_reason":"maxmemory-samples: default fixed to 5 (was 16), CONFIG SET/GET wired to runtime","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-bfp","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-kgn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:20.028538611Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"frankenredis-gess","title":"Record initial performance baselines and check into baselines/ directory","description":"# Initial Baselines

## What
Run the benchmark harness against both FrankenRedis and legacy Redis, record the results, and check them into baselines/.

## Why
The baselines/ directory is empty. Without baselines, there is no way to detect performance regressions or prove improvements. The README claims "profile-proven performance" which requires actual numbers.

## Implementation
1. Build frankenredis in release mode: `cargo build --release -p fr-server`
2. Start frankenredis on port 6399
3. Run fr_benchmark against it with standard workloads:
   - SET only: 100k requests, 50 clients, pipeline 1
   - GET only: 100k requests, 50 clients, pipeline 1 (pre-populate keys first)
   - Mixed 50/50 SET/GET: 100k requests, 50 clients
   - Pipeline depth 16: 100k requests, 50 clients
   - INCR: 100k requests, 50 clients
4. Stop frankenredis, start legacy redis-server on same port
5. Run identical workloads against legacy Redis
6. Record all results as JSON in baselines/:
   - baselines/frankenredis_v0.1.0_set.json
   - baselines/frankenredis_v0.1.0_get.json
   - baselines/frankenredis_v0.1.0_mixed.json
   - baselines/frankenredis_v0.1.0_pipeline16.json
   - baselines/frankenredis_v0.1.0_incr.json
   - baselines/redis_7.2_set.json (comparison anchor)
   - baselines/redis_7.2_get.json
   - etc.
7. Create scripts/record_baselines.sh that automates steps 1-6
8. Create scripts/compare_baselines.py that reads two baseline files and reports deltas

## Validation
- baselines/ contains at least 10 JSON files (5 workloads x 2 servers)
- Each JSON has: ops_sec, p50_us, p95_us, p99_us, p999_us, total_time_sec, total_requests
- FrankenRedis throughput is at least 50% of legacy Redis for basic SET/GET (single-threaded, so parity is expected)
- If FrankenRedis is slower than 50% of Redis, create a follow-up bead to investigate

## Depends On
- Benchmark harness (bxlo) — CLOSED, already implemented

## Files
- baselines/*.json (new, many files)
- scripts/record_baselines.sh (new)
- scripts/compare_baselines.py (new)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:10:23.143539757Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T20:29:52.009288902Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T20:29:52.009108591Z","close_reason":"Recorded initial baselines, added automation scripts, and filed frankenredis-zjii for severe perf gap","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-gess","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-bxlo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-07T03:11:16.601521835Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"frankenredis-ghz","title":"Convert NUM_DATABASES fixed arrays to Vec for runtime database count","description":"## Problem

Store uses `[usize; NUM_DATABASES]` fixed arrays for db_key_counts and db_expires_counts. NUM_DATABASES=16 is a compile-time const. Redis allows configuring database count at startup.

## Implementation Plan

1. Remove `pub const NUM_DATABASES: usize = 16` from fr-store
2. Add `database_count: usize` field to Store, defaulting to 16
3. Change db_key_counts and db_expires_counts to Vec<usize>
4. Initialize both Vecs in Store::new() with `vec![0; 16]`
5. Update all array access to use bounds-checked Vec indexing
6. In SELECT command: validate db < store.database_count (currently checks < NUM_DATABASES)
7. In encode_db_key/decode_db_key: validate db < database_count
8. In SWAPDB: validate both db indices
9. Accept database count as constructor parameter for Store

 {"id":"frankenredis-icsm","title":"EPIC: Establish real performance baseline with redis-benchmark-compatible harness and p50/p95/p99 latency tracking","description":"# Performance Evidence Epic

## Why This Is Critical
The README describes "Deterministic Latency Replication Core (DLRC)" as the "Crown-Jewel Innovation" and claims "profile-proven performance" with "p95/p99 latency tracking." Yet the repo contains ZERO throughput benchmarks, ZERO latency percentile measurements, and an empty baselines/ directory. The only checked-in artifacts measure conformance test execution speed (~1.3ms), not Redis workload performance.

This is the single most dangerous gap because performance is the stated reason this project exists as a differentiator from legacy Redis. Without evidence, DLRC is marketing copy, not engineering.

## What Success Looks Like
- A redis-benchmark-compatible harness that measures ops/sec, latency histograms (p50/p95/p99/p999), and memory usage under mixed workloads
- Baseline numbers checked into baselines/ with comparison against legacy Redis on identical workloads
- CI-gatable regression detection: any commit that degrades p99 by >10% fails the gate
- The README performance claims are either proven or honestly revised

## Architecture
The benchmark harness should be a new binary in fr-server or a dedicated crate that:
1. Connects via real TCP to a running frankenredis instance
2. Runs configurable workloads (SET/GET mixed, pipeline depth, key sizes)
3. Records latency histograms using HdrHistogram or equivalent
4. Outputs JSON artifacts compatible with the existing artifacts/ structure
5. Compares against baselines/ and reports deltas

## Dependencies
All sub-tasks feed into this epic.  {"id":"frankenredis-kgn","title":"[EPIC] Dynamic Server Configuration — Wire CONFIG SET to Runtime Enforcement","description":"## Overview

FrankenRedis has numerous hardcoded constants that Redis makes configurable via CONFIG SET/GET. While the config table in fr-runtime advertises these parameters, the actual enforcement code uses compile-time constants, creating a correctness gap where CONFIG SET appears to succeed but has no effect on server behavior.

## Why This Matters

1. **Observability**: Clients that read CONFIG GET or INFO to verify configuration see stale/wrong values
2. **Drop-in parity**: Redis operators expect CONFIG SET maxclients/hz/timeout to take effect immediately
3. **Security**: The bind address defaulting to 0.0.0.0 while config says 127.0.0.1 is a security hole
4. **Testing**: Integration tests that change config at runtime get wrong behavior

## Scope

This epic covers wiring the following runtime-configurable parameters from CONFIG SET through to their actual enforcement points:
- maxclients (server connection limit)
- hz (event loop frequency)
- client-query-buffer-limit / proto-max-bulk-len
- client-output-buffer-limit
- repl-backlog-size
- repl-timeout / repl-ping-replica-period
- busy-reply-threshold (lua-time-limit)
- maxmemory-samples
- bind address

## Architecture Note

The challenge is that CONFIG SET runs in fr-runtime, but enforcement happens in fr-server (connection acceptance, buffer limits, timeouts). We need a communication channel — either shared state on ServerState that fr-server reads each tick, or a config-change event queue.

## Evidence of the problem

INFO hz/configured_hz was hardcoded to 10 (fixed in commit f8c5a8a). INFO maxclients was hardcoded to 10000 (fixed). But the actual enforcement in fr-server still uses compile-time MAX_CLIENTS=10000, QUERY_BUFFER_LIMIT=1MB, etc. CONFIG SET changes these in the config table but the server never reads the updated values.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:13.544766479Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:35:06.113114065Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:35:06.111623533Z","close_reason":"Epic acknowledged — children tasks now unblocked for implementation","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankenredis-klvg","title":"Forward BGREWRITEAOF from dispatch_argv to runtime via Store flag","description":"# Forward BGREWRITEAOF from dispatch_argv to runtime via Store flag

## Problem
BGREWRITEAOF in fr-command returns the success message without actually writing. The runtime has the real implementation. Lua redis.call(\"BGREWRITEAOF\") is a no-op.

## Design
Add a pub bgrewriteaof_requested: bool flag to Store. When fr-command's bgrewriteaof_cmd is called, set this flag to true and return the success message. The runtime checks this flag after each dispatch and performs the actual AOF rewrite.

## Implementation
1. Add pub bgrewriteaof_requested: bool to Store (default false)
2. fr-command bgrewriteaof_cmd sets store.bgrewriteaof_requested = true
3. Runtime checks store.bgrewriteaof_requested after execute_frame and calls persist_snapshot_to_disk
4. Clear the flag after handling

## Why this pattern
Same as DEBUG RELOAD — the fr-command layer can signal intent, the runtime fulfills it. This is the cleanest cross-layer communication without restructuring dispatch_argv's signature.

## Testing
- Integration test: verify AOF file is written when BGREWRITEAOF called from Lua
- Conformance: existing BGREWRITEAOF test should still pass

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (flag field)
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (set flag)

 {"id":"frankenredis-kzxw","title":"Implement ACL SAVE/LOAD file persistence","description":"# Implement ACL SAVE/LOAD file persistence

## What
ACL SAVE and ACL LOAD currently return OK without persisting. They should:
- ACL SAVE: serialize all ACL user rules to the ACL file (path from config)
- ACL LOAD: read and apply ACL rules from the ACL file, replacing current rules

## Design
Redis stores ACL rules in a text file, one user per line, in the same format as ACL SETUSER:
```
user default on ~* &* +@all
user admin on >password ~* &* +@all
user readonly on >pass ~* &* +@read -@write
```

## Implementation plan
1. Add pub acl_file_path: Option<PathBuf> to Runtime's ServerState (configured via CONFIG SET aclfile)
2. Add fn serialize_acl_rules(&self) -> String to AuthState that outputs one line per user
3. Add fn load_acl_rules(&mut self, content: &str) -> Result<(), String> to AuthState that parses lines
4. ACL SAVE: write serialize_acl_rules() to acl_file_path atomically (temp file + rename)
5. ACL LOAD: read acl_file_path, call load_acl_rules(), return OK or error
6. If acl_file_path is None, return "ERR There is no configured ACL file" (matches Redis)

## Serialization format
Each user line: `user <name> <on|off> [>password...] [~pattern...] [&pattern...] [+command|-command|+@cat|-@cat|allcommands|nocommands|allkeys|allchannels|reset]`

## Key details
- ACL LOAD replaces ALL users except "default" (which is always reset first then reconfigured)
- If parsing fails on any line, ACL LOAD rolls back to previous state and returns error
- Atomic file writes: write to .tmp then rename (already established pattern in fr-persist)

## Testing
- Unit test: serialize → load round-trip preserves all user rules
- Unit test: ACL LOAD with invalid content returns error and preserves previous state
- Conformance: ACL SAVE + ACL LOAD round-trip test

 {"id":"frankenredis-n5xv","title":"Implement incremental store digest (O(1) per command)","description":"# Implement Incremental Store Digest (O(1) per command)

## What

Store::state_digest() (crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs:7875-7928) walks every entry
in the database, hashing keys, values (including per-element for hashes/lists/
sets/zsets/streams), and expiry times. This is O(N_keys * avg_value_size).

After the April 9 lazy-digest refactor (frankenredis-zjii), this function is no
longer called on the hot path — it's deferred to inside record_threat_event(),
which only fires when emit_evidence_ledger=true AND a threat event occurs (parse
error, auth denial, slow command, etc.). The success path pays zero cost.

However, when a threat DOES fire, the full O(N) scan still happens. At 1M keys,
this can take 10-100ms, blocking the event loop during a security-critical event
recording. This is exactly when you want the server to respond quickly.

## Why

Replace the full-scan implementation with a running digest that's maintained
incrementally on every store mutation. Each insert/update/delete XORs out the
old entry's hash and XORs in the new one. The digest is always O(1) to read.

## Design

Add a `digest: u64` field to Store. On every mutation:

```rust
fn update_digest_for_entry(&mut self, key: &[u8], old_entry: Option<&Entry>, new_entry: Option<&Entry>) {
    if let Some(old) = old_entry {
        self.digest ^= entry_hash(key, old);
    }
    if let Some(new) = new_entry {
        self.digest ^= entry_hash(key, new);
    }
}
```

Where entry_hash computes FNV-1a over key + type_tag + value_bytes + expiry.
XOR is commutative and associative, so order doesn't matter. The running
digest matches what a full scan would produce (with the XOR-based algorithm
rather than the current sequential FNV chain).

## Mutation Points to Instrument

Every path that modifies Store::entries needs to call update_digest_for_entry:
- insert (set, hset, lpush, sadd, zadd, xadd, etc.)
- update (append, incr, setrange, etc.)
- delete (del, expire eviction, pop, etc.)
- bulk operations (flushdb, rename, copy)
- expiry changes (expire, pexpire, persist, etc.)

This is a broad surface area. The key correctness invariant:
self.digest == self.state_digest_full_scan() at all times.
Add a debug assertion that checks this periodically (e.g., every 1000 mutations
in debug builds).

## Dependency on BTreeMap→HashMap Migration

If frankenredis-hs04 (BTreeMap→HashMap) lands first and changes state_digest to
use XOR-of-entry-hashes (order-independent), this bead becomes much simpler —
just maintain the XOR accumulator incrementally rather than recomputing it.

If this bead lands first, state_digest must be changed to XOR-based anyway for
incrementality, which is a prerequisite for the HashMap migration's digest
compatibility. So either ordering works, but hs04-first is cleaner.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Store has a `running_digest: u64` field updated on every mutation
2. state_digest() returns the running value in O(1)
3. Debug assertions verify digest consistency every N mutations
4. All 43 conformance tests pass
5. Threat events still record correct digests
6.  {"id":"frankenredis-yc3","title":"Implement lexical scoping / upvalue capture in Lua evaluator","description":"## Problem

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 2274: When calling a LuaValue::Function, a completely fresh environment is created (Env::new()). This means closures cannot access variables from their defining scope.

Example that breaks:
  local x = 10
  local f = function() return x end
  return f()  -- returns nil, should return 10

This is a FUNDAMENTAL semantic bug that silently breaks any Lua code using closures, callbacks, or higher-order functions.

## How Real Lua Does It

Lua 5.1 uses upvalue capture: when a function is created, it captures references to local variables in enclosing scopes. These captured variables (upvalues) travel with the function closure.

## Implementation Options

### Option A: Environment Chain (simpler)
When creating a LuaValue::Function, snapshot the current environment as a parent chain. When the function is called, create a new env with the captured parent as the lookup fallback.

Data model change:
- LuaValue::Function currently stores (name, body_statements)
- Change to store (name, body_statements, captured_env: Option<Env>)
- In Env::get(), if local lookup fails, walk the captured_env chain

### Option B: Explicit Upvalue Tracking (more correct)
Track which variables are referenced by the function body. Only capture those specific bindings. More memory-efficient but harder to implement.

## Recommendation

Option A is much simpler and sufficient for Redis Lua scripts (which are typically not deeply nested). The performance cost is minimal since Env is just a HashMap.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: LuaValue::Function variant, Env struct, call_function, function definition handling

## Testing

- Test: local variable access from closure
- Test: nested closures (closure inside closure)
- Test: closure over loop variable
- Test: multiple closures sharing same upvalue
- Test: redis.call inside a closure","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:10.287677089Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T05:22:00.978183370Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T05:22:00.977708791Z","close_reason":"Implemented upvalue capture via captured_env on LuaFunc.  {"id":"frankenredis-yys0","title":"Precompute ACL category->command mapping in OnceLock","description":"# Precompute ACL Category→Command Mapping in OnceLock

## What

command_categories() in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:62 and
commands_in_acl_category() in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:8924 recompute
ACL category→command mappings from scratch on every call. Each call iterates
21 ACL categories × the entire COMMAND_TABLE (~200 commands) and does
split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<&str>>() on each command's flags string —
~4,200 Vec allocations per invocation.

After the April 9 short-circuit (frankenredis-zjii), the DEFAULT user (no
category rules) never hits this code path. But any user with ACL category
rules (e.g., +@read -@dangerous) still pays the full cost on every command.

## Why This Matters

Production Redis deployments commonly use ACL categories for least-privilege
security. A web app might configure: user webuser +@read +@write -@dangerous.
That user has non-empty allowed_categories, so the short-circuit doesn't apply,
and every command pays ~4,200 allocations for category resolution.

## Design

Use OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> to build the mapping
once at startup:

```rust
static ACL_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();

fn commands_in_acl_category_cached(category: &str) -> &'static [&'static str] {
    let map = ACL_CATEGORY_MAP.get_or_init(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        for &cat in ACL_CATEGORIES {
            m.insert(cat, commands_in_acl_category_uncached(cat));
        }
        m
    });
    map.get(category).map(|v| v.as_slice()).unwrap_or(&[])
}
```

Also precompute the reverse mapping (command→categories) so
command_categories() is a single HashMap lookup:

```rust
static COMMAND_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. commands_in_acl_category uses OnceLock-cached mapping
2. command_categories uses OnceLock-cached reverse mapping
3. Zero per-command allocations for ACL category resolution
4. All ACL conformance tests pass
5. Users with non-trivial ACL rules see no throughput regression vs default user
6. Memory overhead is bounded (21 categories × 200 commands = ~4200 pointers = ~33KB)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:29:12.934372724Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T21:39:17.081568068Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T21:39:17.081295221Z","close_reason":"Cached ACL category mappings + command reverse map","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
